desc: Galway is one of the easiest places to build a west-of-Ireland trip around: trad sessions in the Latin Quarter, sea air and long walks in Salthill, buses and tours into Connemara, and weather-dependent island days when the Aran connections line up. It is lively without being huge, but it is rarely cheap or quiet once festival season and summer weekends kick in.

todo.name: Latin Quarter
todo.desc: Follow live music between pubs, but start early for seats in smaller traditional venues.

todo.name: Salthill Promenade
todo.desc: Walk to Salthill for Galway Bay views and the local kick-the-wall turnaround ritual.

todo.name: Aran Islands day trip
todo.desc: Use a ferry or flight connection on a calm day for stone forts, bikes and Atlantic scenery.

todo.name: Connemara day trip
todo.desc: Head west for bog, mountains and coastline when weather gives you a window.

todo.name: Blackrock and the Salthill Prom
todo.desc: Walk out for bay views, sea-swimming energy and the classic kick-the-wall finish at the end of the promenade.

todo.name: Spanish Arch and Corrib edge
todo.desc: Use the river and harbour edge to connect Galway's medieval core with its more open waterfront feel.

wifi.note: Good in most city hotels, pubs and cafes, but island excursions, Connemara drives and some older stone venues are easier if you keep mobile data in reserve.

prac.fltNote: Galway has no commercial airport. Shannon and Ireland West Airport Knock are the closest useful gateways, while Dublin works well if you are happy to finish with an intercity coach or rail-plus-coach leg; Irish Citylink runs frequent non-stop Galway-Dublin Airport services. Festival dates, bank-holiday weekends and summer west-coast road trips tend to drive costs more than any one flight route does.
prac.visa: The Common Travel Area applies for British and Irish citizens; other travellers should check Ireland entry rules separately from the UK.
alerts: Festival weekends sell out far in advance and can push room rates up hard.
Atlantic rain and wind can cancel or reshape Aran and Connemara plans at short notice.
Late-night taxis can be scarce after busy pub nights.
Central accommodation can be noisy when live-music venues spill out late.

sweet: May, June and September are the best balance for music, daylight and west-coast side trips, while July and August are livelier but more expensive and more weather-fragile for island plans.
